1.4 Ambition Advance beyond the state-of-the-art and the extent to which our proposed work is ambitious The use of bio-based materials is a very interesting way to decrease the environmental impact of end users and packaging industries. However, improvements in the functionality and properties of bio-based materials are required to fill the performance and price gap with fossil materials that generally hampers their replacement. In this sense, our BIOnTOP project has the ambition to develop novel packaging products in different formats (trays, films, flowpacks, and textiles) using new copolymers based on PLA and other bio-based diacids as well as innovative barrier coatings that will lead to significant knowledge advancements of the state-of-the-art in packaging materials with tailored end-of-life phase as follows: State of the art in the field of home and industrially compostability and degradation under anaerobic conditions Industrial compostability was the first process of organic recycling for which a test strategy including specific requirements was developed. This resulted in the harmonized EU standard EN 13432 Requirements for packaging recoverable through composting and biodegradation - Test scheme and evaluation criteria for the final acceptance of packaging (2000) according to which a broad range of products are certified. However, during home composting, the high temperatures (> 50 °C) required by some polymers such as PLA for starting the degradation and obtained during industrial composting processes are mostly not reached. At lower temperatures, the degradation rate is reduced or even limited. Furthermore, in order to be home compostable, a material must demonstrate sufficient biodegradation and disintegration at ambient temperature. This principle was first applied by the OK compost HOME Conformity Mark of certification institute TÜV AUSTRIA Belgium. Currently, also the German Certification Institute has developed a logo: DIN-Geprüft Home Compostable. On a European level, no standard has been established yet for home compostable products, but recently home composting has become an important factor in France, with the ban of conventional lightweight plastic shopping bags, the encouragement of bioplastics compostable at the domestic level, and the introduction of a French national standard on home compostable materials NF T 51-800 Plastics – Specifications for plastic suitable for home composting (2015). Moreover, several European companies, such as BASF, BIOTEC, LIMAGRAIN, NOVAMONT, FUTAMURA, etc. have developed polymer resins that are home compostable, but they have limited thermomechanical behavior and processability. Most of these products are starch, polyhydroxy butyrate (PHB), or polyhydroxy succinate (PBS) based, although the Ecovio compounds of BASF contain PLA (in low amount when meeting home compostability requirements - rest being non-bio-based PBAT). On the market, there is still a lack of products meeting the needs of the packaging applications (in terms of price, controlled biodegradability, and product properties). Moreover, it should be noted that at present, PLA can be in the material formula (although it is not degrading at ambient temperature), as the current test requires that 90% absolute or relative to cellulose biodegradation is reached. However, in line with the International and American specifications for industrial compostable products and DIN-Geprüft Industrial Compostable label, in which it is requested that organic components between 1% and 10% must be tested separately, it is likely that the European standard in discussion follows a similar approach. In that case, materials including even minor PLA amounts and currently claiming home compostability would fail. In this case, the use of our BIOnTOP copolymers would become mandatory. Besides, it is well known that the compostability rate is thickness dependent. While it may be possible to compost a specific bioplastic sample used in packaging film, a consumer product such as a thick injected cosmetic jar, made from this bioplastic may not compost at a rate sufficient to meet the compostability standard, simply because it is too thick. This is true even for starch-derived or PLA-based materials at high thickness used for rigid food packaging (trays). For example, PLA for industrial composting conditions is certified up to 2000 µm thickness (Total-Corbion), but polybutylene succinate (PBS) up to 68 µm (Xinjian Blue Ridge Tunhe Polyester Co., LTD; China) and polybutylene adipate terephthalate (PBAT) up to 120 µm (BASF, Germany). As biodegradation is also determined by the characteristics of the environment (temperature, moisture content, pH, oxygen presence, microbial population…), specific test procedures were developed to determine biodegradation of plastics under anaerobic conditions (ISO 15985). However, although AD in biogasification plants is considered in the EU Waste Directive as a valuable treatment method for organic recycling, there is currently no standard existing that defines the specific requirements on biodegradation, disintegration, and effect on the digestate that should be fulfilled (similar to EN 13432 for industrial composting). AD has so far only been poorly investigated as an EoL option for bioplastics and packaging. Still, it is an interesting technology due to additional biogas formation, suitable for electricity or heat production. In fact, the bioplastics can have a positive effect on biogas production due to their high C/N ratio. The results of our project will help to understand the behavior of plastics and packaging in these anaerobic systems and can be a basis for the development of such a standard. Beyond the state of the art, we will combine several technologies discussed below to reach modular EoL that are currently not achievable with the available materials under controlled waste management scenarios, while still ensuring the material meets the properties required for packaging productions. We will improve the biodegradation of PLA-based blends and copolymers in home composting conditions, allowing us to apply a higher PLA content and more cost-effective products. Moreover, the behavior of PLA products in AD systems will be evaluated, opening new EoL options, in both cases contributing with test results towards new harmonized standards. State of the art in the field of PLA, lactide (LD), and lactic acid (LA) based copolymers Among the biodegradable polymers, polylactic acid (PLA)-based polymers are one of the most extensively studied biomass-derived synthetic polymers, used for a wide range of applications covering disposable household items, food packaging, agricultural films, drug delivery systems, and implantable biomedical devices. However, some PLA properties are still insufficient for their breakthrough in more potential applications, mainly due to its lack of toughness and impact resistance. Generally, commercial grades of PLA are co-polymers of L-LA and D-LA or the corresponding lactide (LD) forms. The ratio of L to D enantiomers is known to affect the properties of PLA. Thus, the content of D-LA affects the glass transition of PLA, which decreases progressively, as well as the melting temperature and the crystallization rate, which decrease with the increase in the D units content. In general, in industrial packaging applications, the use of PLA with a content of D units of 2–6 wt. % randomly distributed is very common as then, the crystallization of PLA is low, favoring the production of amorphous transparent films (in contrast to textiles needing high crystallinity). The crystallinity of PLA interferes with controlled degradation and is an obstacle to the application of PLA as a biodegradable plastic. Therefore, our research aims to equip PLA-based polymers with controllable biodegradation profiles, tailored mechanical properties, reactivity for chemical modification, and other functionalities while keeping the favorable characteristics of PLA. In that sense, a promising approach is the introduction of hydrophilic (reactive) segments, other bio-based monomers, and/or branched structures into PLA. Currently, the lack of consistency in biodegradability studies, with varying conditions and protocols being applied to different systems, makes it very hard and controversial to compare the biodegradability of plastics and in particular of PLA and derived materials developed in the academic literature. Biodegradation of PLA As seen previously, the biodegradation of plastics is a complex process involving multiple steps and pathways and is highly dependent on the conditions. The initial degradation step for PLA is to be broken down into monomers or low molecular weight oligomers, where the ester bonds are cleaved hydrolytically to facilitate uptake into microorganisms. PLA becomes water soluble when the molecular weight (Mn) is below 20,000 g/mol. The initial degradation into smaller fragments is a rate-limiting step in biodegradation processes. After uptake into the microorganisms, subsequent metabolic activity breaks the polymers down into metabolic end products such as carbon dioxide and water, while a portion of the carbon is converted into biomass. Composting of PLA proceeds efficiently under adequate conditions due to the combined effects of hydrolysis and microbial activity. Furthermore, elevated temperatures encountered during composting accelerate the hydrolysis process of PLA, especially when temperatures exceed 50ºC. Extracellular enzymes released by specific microorganisms can cause cleavage of PLA chains, which may contribute to the degradation process. However, some studies have shown that extracellular enzymes do not significantly accelerate depolymerization. The reaction mechanism for the hydrolysis of polyesters is a bimolecular nucleophilic substitution reaction (SN2), which may be catalyzed with either acids or bases. Furthermore, the reaction rates for hydrolytic degradation are dependent on a number of factors: temperature, size and shape of the sample, molecular weight (Mw), crystallinity, and relative humidity. Moreover, polymers that crosslink during irradiation tend to show reduced biodegradation because the higher Mw decreases the ability for uptake by microorganisms. PLA synthesis The synthesis routines of PLA can be divided into polycondensation reaction from LA and ring opening polymer

1.4 Ambition We recognize the increasing need in the twenty-first century to develop stronger, tougher, and more chemically resistant structural materials in order to meet the challenges arising from the rapid increase in demanding requirements across a wide and diverse range of applications, from construction to transportation, health to energy [6]. Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMCs) are of great interest as thermo-structural materials [7] and were developed initially for military and aerospace applications. Some of the most important market niches include: thermally loaded components in space applications such as propulsion systems or thermal protection systems [8–10], refractory materials for furnaces [11,12] and energy reactors [13,14], and ceramic brake systems [15]. Emerging sectors comprise: antiballistic plates [16], gas burners, high pressure heat exchangers [17], fuel and solar cells [18]. Currently, we are introducing them into many other new fields, and their range of applications will grow if their manufacturing costs can be significantly reduced [19]. The global CMCs market was valued at US $ 4,857.6 million in 2018 and is projected to reach US $ 11,516.1 million by 2026, growing at a CAGR of 11.4% from 2019 to 2026, helped in particular by the demand from the aerospace sector. We can segment them into non-oxide and oxide CMCs [20], with the former offering higher temperature resistance (typically up to 1400 – 1600°C) and the latter intrinsically oxidation resistant, thus finding applications where this is a critical factor, albeit their maximum use temperature is generally around 1000 – 1200°C. The aforementioned applications will significantly fuel the growth of the CMCs market. However, their cost has been a concern for many industries over the last decade. The prices of ceramic matrix composites are controlled, to a large extent, by the costs of ceramic fibers and energy-intensive processing routes. Nonetheless, substantial opportunities will be presented by the energy and power sector, as it is a rapidly growing application segment [21]. 

1.4.1 Advances beyond the state of the art 

1.4.1.1 Processing routes of non-oxide CMCs 
To date, we have focused the greatest amount of developmental work on the non-oxide CMCs and, in particular, carbon (C) and silicon carbide (SiC) fiber reinforced SiC-based materials (Cf/SiC and SiCf/SiC composites respectively), since non-oxide CMCs have attractive high temperature properties, including creep resistance, microstructural stability, excellent mechanical and thermal properties. We commonly fabricate CMCs reinforced with long fibers by infiltration methods [1], in which the ceramic matrix is formed from a fluid infiltrating into the fiber structure. These techniques differ from each other in the types of fluids and the processes for converting the fluid into a ceramic: 
• Chemical vapor infiltration (CVI): infiltration using a preceramic gaseous precursor (a vapor), which yields a ceramic as a result of chemical decomposition at elevated temperature; 
• Polymer infiltration and pyrolysis (PIP): infiltration with a low viscosity preceramic organo-metallic polymer followed by pyrolysis when the polymer converts into a ceramic (multiple infiltration-pyrolysis cycles are required to reach the desired density); 
• Reactive melt infiltration (RMI): infiltration with a liquid metal, which converts into a ceramic when reacting with a surrounding substance (e.g. liquid silicon infiltration, LSI, a type of RMI utilizing molten silicon). 

Generally, we employ the following fabrication stages in all infiltration techniques: 
− Preform preparation: At this stage, we lay up and mold the fiber reinforcing phase to form a preform of the required shape; it can be made of stacked 2D layers of fabrics, needled ‘2.5D’ layers, or woven 3D fabric layers; 
− Interphase deposition: Where required, we may deposit a coating (called interphase) over the fiber surfaces before or after preform fabrication; 
− Infiltration: We infiltrate the reinforcing fiber preform with a preceramic fluid as outlined above; 
− Thermal processing: The preceramic fluid incorporated into the fiber reinforcing structure converts into a ceramic, filling the space between the fibers. 

In CMCs, the fiber-matrix interfacial domain is a decisive component controlling the (thermo-)mechanical performance of these materials [22,23]. The favored damage-tolerant fracture behavior and high fracture toughness are achieved by a weak bonding between the fiber and the matrix [24], while this weak interface needs to be strong enough to have sufficient load sharing between fiber and matrix. This load sharing is important to achieve a high strength of the CMC materials [25]. In non-oxide CMCs, we adjust the interfacial domain by applying coatings, so-called “interphases,” on the fiber [26]. Although many materials have been tested as fiber coatings [27], we have only achieved long-term stable damage-tolerant fracture behavior in non-oxide CMCs by using BN (boron nitride) or PyC (pyrolytic carbon) as interphases. A drawback of both materials is their limited oxidation and corrosion resistance; if the interphase corrodes via oxidation, the performance of the CMC degrades. To overcome this issue, we apply additional layer(s) on top of the BN or PyC layers [28]. These additional layer(s) (e.g. SiC, Si3N4, etc.) protect the underlying BN or PyC layer during further CMC processing and, to a limited degree, also during the use of the CMC [29]. Moreover, these layers provide an additional possibility to adjust the bonding of the interface coating system to the matrix [26]. 

Applying the coatings on every single filament of a tow consisting of several hundreds of filaments with high quality is a great challenge. The coatings need to be homogeneous with a high degree of coverage. At the same time, we need to avoid bridging between the filaments in order to be able to further process the coated tows by techniques such as filament winding. We can apply the fiber coatings by various techniques, among which Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) and Wet-chemical deposition are the most common. We compare the advantages and disadvantages of both CVD and wet-chemical coating technologies in Table 1.3. 

Table 1.3 - Comparison of advantages and disadvantages of coating ceramic fibers by CVD and wet-chemical coatings technology. 

| CVD route | Wet-chemical route |
|-----------|--------------------|
| Advantages | Advantages |
| • Doping of coatings relatively simple | • No additional pyrolysis steps necessary |
| • Relatively low thermal load on fiber | • High variety of material systems can be deposited |
| • No synthesis of precursors needed | • Low investment costs |
| • Coating set-up simple to modify | • High degree of flexibility for process design |
| • High process speed and through-put possible | • Low maintenance costs |
| • Technology easy to upscale and to implement in industrial production | |
| Disadvantages | Disadvantages |
| • Limitations in material systems that can be deposited | • Very slow process (~several weeks) |
| • Raw materials often expensive | • High residual porosity (10-15%) due to bridging |
| • High investment costs for the technology | • High capital and production costs |
| • Low process speeds, low through-put | • Multiple infiltration-pyrolysis cycles |

| • Use of corrosive gases | • Low maximum linear elastic levels |
| • Shrinkage, crystallization or phase transformations of the coatings during CMC processing or use possible | • High temperature and highly corrosive metal melt (may damage fibers) |
| • Modification of coating materials needs modification of synthesis of precursors | • Residual free silicon |
| • Higher thermal load on fibers during pyrolysis of the coatings | • Low mechanical properties |
| • Rough surfaces need machining with diamond tools | |

The fiber-matrix bonding must be sufficiently weak to allow debonding and consequently the relaxation of the stress field at the crack tip and the bridging of the matrix cracks by the fibers [30]. There are two methods of achieving this; for dense matrix CMCs (e.g. SiC-based composites), we apply a low cohesion interphase coating (e.g. pyrolytic carbon or boron nitride) to the fibers before the matrix is infiltrated. CMCs of this type are called Weak Interface Composites (WICs), and toughening mechanisms such as crack deflection at the fiber/matrix interface, crack-bridging by fibers, and fiber pullout at the crack plane are operative. An alternative approach (e.g. for oxide-based composites) is to ensure that the matrix is weak. Such Weak Matrix Composites (WMCs) are characterized by the matrix being porous; the reduced matrix stiffness and strength allows damage-tolerant behavior, even in the case of a strong matrix-fiber interface. While both approaches work, for WICs, the interface coatings may oxidize at high temperatures in air leading to brittle failure, while WMCs typically display a low overall strength or, if used at excessively high temperatures, the matrix can further densify leading to brittle behavior.

1.4 Ambition  
We aim at overcoming obstacles from current recycling technologies by designing coating formulations with smart additives, which allow adequate sorting, separation, and recycling. Developments of separation and recycling technologies will be investigated, and new separation technologies utilizing novel mechanical and chemical recycling procedures will be validated to meet the EU's circular economy and environmental targets.  

1.4.1 Progress beyond state-of-the-art  
Building further on the available knowledge, expertise, and patented IP within our consortium, we will research and develop a debonding system based on triggerable additives, mechanical recycling, chemical recycling (Creasolv process), and the latest coating (monitoring) technology. We will investigate two debonding systems: (i) a single layer with the trigger inside and (ii) a two-layer system consisting of a standard coating and a ‘primer’ layer (see sketches). Note that ‘primer’ is used here as a common name for tie layer or primer or base layer. For selected combinations of the coating-substrate materials, we will research several triggers and several options for the actual removal of the coating (process conditions, 1-step versus 2-step). The output will be a portfolio of debonding strategies, mentioning for each of the solutions the pros and cons. This novel information will form the basis for our demonstration of the circular use of coated parts but also be made available to the wider stakeholder community for further use. In the next paragraphs, we describe the concrete progress beyond the state-of-the-art that we aim for (i) triggerable materials, (ii) optical monitoring for process optimization and sorting of the coated waste, (iii) actual recycling, and (iv) eco-design aspects.  

(i) Triggerable materials and related debonding systems – mainly related to WP1 & WP2 work  
Coatings that debond on demand lose their adhesive function when an external trigger is applied. We researched a novel separation layer for textiles by embedding thermally expandable syntactic microspheres (in FP7 project ECOMETEX). The separation concept was shown on a lab scale in boiling water, which is not an efficient process as it needs extensive energy (heating, drying). We developed an innovative, patented (see section 1.4.2) interfacial debonding adhesive technology (INDAR™ Inside) permitting the dismantling of structural aerospace assemblies without damage and was used for selective thermal stripping of aircraft paints. We have developed a variety of ‘smart’ nano- and microcapsules based on various materials (ceramics, metals, polymers) that react to external stimuli (water, mechanical stresses, microorganisms, etc.), in projects LORCENCIS and BYEFOULING (see also project table above). We developed additives for interaction with microwaves. Based on that, a unique patented (polyester) yarn was developed, which disintegrates during microwaving. This yarn enables easy and inexpensive removal of e.g. zippers (disassembly step).  

DECOAT novelty: Using the available IP and expertise, we will develop triggerable additives for water-based coating formulations with a lowered debonding temperature as compared to existing procedures, although exceeding the processing and working temperature required/set by industrial demo cases. A sufficient difference between the manufacturing, maximal service, and the debonding temperature will be ensured and will be realized by improving the heat direction towards the additives, e.g. by heat focusing layers or thermal conductive paints. Thermo-responsive core-shell nanostructured co-polymers, Super Absorbent Polymers (SAP), and novel hybrid microwave susceptible materials (based on metal nanoparticles, silicon carbides, and multiwall carbon nanotubes MWCNTs) will be investigated. Separation will be done by heat sources (like steam, microwaves, and IR) that are available at the industrial level to avoid the need for additional, or only very limited, drying. To reach a clean surface after debonding, we focus on controlled interfacial decohesion. For detection purposes, fluorescent molecules will be added to the “smart” additives (dyes). The table below gives an overview of the materials to be developed and the polymer coating matrices to which they can be added. Already a first matching between the trigger mechanism and the polymer matrix is made, based on the chemistry of the particles and what is available in literature. As mentioned, this is a first matching; further investigation within our project is needed. Below the table, each of the triggerable materials is discussed, giving more info on their synthesis and the challenges for their use in an actual debonding system.  

Triggerable materials  
Coating Polymer matrix Triggers  
Thermo-responsive core-shell nanostructured co-polymers Core: e.g. poly(propyl methacrylate) or poly(butyl methacrylate) Shell: e.g. poly(4-vinylpyridine-co-methacrylic acid), ethylene-glycol-bismethacrylate, Functionalized with: organic dye (I), iron oxides (supermagnetic nanoparticles) Acrylic, PU PVC Heat / Steam (Temperature)

Blowing agents (INDAR) Blowing agents: azodicarbonamide and polycarboxylic acid Blended into formulations for primers, combination with other triggers Epoxies, PU Heat / Steam (Temperature)  

Thermo-responsive core-shell nanostructured co-polymers  
We have vast experience in synthesis and development of tailored microcapsules and nanoadditives with functional properties (currently at TRL3). We have built over the years a strong IP portfolio of microcapsules for various applications.  

DECOAT novelty: We will make progress on the following topics to realize this triggerable material:  
Microcapsule core: selection of polymers with thermo-responsive properties or inclusion of active ingredients that help delamination of textile layers (e.g. poly(propyl methacrylate) or poly(butyl methacrylate)). Microcapsule shell: selection of suitable shell components to enclose the core material. In thermo-responsive core-shell nanostructured co-polymers, a protective thermoplastic shell with tunable glass transition temperature (Tg) and melting temperature (Tm) will be tailored to have a higher Tg than the core’s polymer. The Tg is highly related to segmental polymer chain motion. As polymers warm up, the shell with the high Tg will act as a protective coating for the polymeric core, which will be in melting conditions at elevated temperature. When the ambient temperature surpasses the Tg of the shell, the inner core (material) will have a high chain mobility enabling detaching of the surfaces. At this defined moment, the core material is released from the capsule, triggering debonding. On the one hand, the shells should not react with the core material, and on the other hand, they should have a good chemical compatibility with the surrounding matrices. Examples of possible materials are: melamine resins, polyurea/polyurethanes, polyacrylates. In addition, the shells should provide a good storage for the active ingredients during the entire lifetime of the products and only rupture/or release the active ingredient when triggered (end of the product life). Functionalization of the protective thermoplastic shell to increase its compatibility with a wide range of ‘coating’ formulations, also for INDAR technology (see below). The main materials: PU, PVC, acrylic, epoxies. Of potential further interest: amino, cyanates, isocyanides, hydroxyls, sulphonamides, and phthalates. We are aware that some forms of these substances have restrictions, so REACH compliance will be checked (see also Task 6.1).  

The use of the microcapsules in the debonding system: The microcapsule concentration (ca. 5% or less) and cross-linking density of the adhesive will be tailored and optimized for strong adhesion during product use but also for ease of debonding at end-of-life. The latter will be achieved by exposure to heat as this will melt the shell, and thus activate the core. Homogeneous heat dissipation and activation of the microcapsules will be obtained by adding superparamagnetic nanoparticles (Fe3O4). Application of irradiation will locally (=near the microcapsules) raise the temperature. The aim is to develop materials that will be able to create ‘soft flexible points’ inside the crosslinked adhesive network in the presence of the external stimuli, thus improving detachment.  

Summarizing, the trigger mechanism for the thermo-responsive core-shell nanostructured co-polymers relies on the different physical properties (Tg) between the core and the shell materials that construct the primer layer. The induction of heat onto the core-shell materials above the Tg of the organic shell results in the different polymer chain mobility enhancement between the core and the shell. The outcome is the reduction of the attachment between the primer layer and both the top coating and substrate, enabling the detachment of the top coating from the corresponding substrate.

1.4 Ambition  
We will bring to bear a truly “Disruptive Business Model” that FMCG companies fear and want at the same time. It requires digital developments that ultimately enable materials supply to be more distributed than today and in a more open innovation format that allows consumers to design and customise their own products. This will deliver an unprecedented scale of “designed for me” and “Sustainability” in the FMCG sector, with less waste/transport/CO2 emission and improved efficiency. Our digital platform combined with our fablab services will be the first integrated digital infrastructure available for non-FMCP experts or consumers to design and manufacture their own particulate/liquid FMCG based on their individual preferences. The nearest current state-of-the-art to our approach are Function of Beauty that has built a software platform to determine precise formulas for both shampoo and conditioner for every customer and Proven that uses data from the world’s largest beauty database and machine learning algorithms to personalize skincare products. Also, paint customisation services are offered by manufacturers (e.g. AkzoNobel), supply chain stakeholders (e.g. Riolett Custom Aerosols) and retailers (e.g. B&Q, Halfords). We surpass the paint and the beauty customisation approaches for the following reasons:  
● Paint customisation services are only really to customise colour and it is not possible to request other features such as a custom sheen (gloss) level. Moreover, paint customisation services only apply to a limited product range (essentially broad wall emulsions). We will provide unrivalled customisation/personalization for a wider range of soft matter FMCG.  
● We provide open source tools (via our digital platform) that allow anyone without FMCG formulation expertise to digitally design and test the performance of personalised/customised formulations. Paint and beauty formulation knowledge is currently locked in proprietary manufacturers’ formulation modelling capabilities or databases that are not necessarily suited for use by non-experts or consumers. We will provide a user-friendly digital knowledge management open access resource that displays relevant formulation ingredient information and ensures compatibility of modelling and simulation with different process information systems, enabling anyone to formulate new safe and environmentally benign products.  
● Our platform capability that allows manufacturers to plug-in their models (e.g. soft matter, process, LCA/LCCA) for consumers, DIYers and SMEs to use for creating bespoke formulations is a key differentiator of our project. Formulation computational modelling including a series of numerical methods will forecast how the formulation will perform in the consumer home, reducing the testing procedures and avoiding a bad experience to the consumer. Formulation process digital twin will virtually create products, mimicking the real equipment performance. The digital twin will take advantage of CFD tools to digitally analyse and optimize the formulation processes.  
● Our big data analytics/AI capability would allow for real-time tracking and innovation response to trends in consumer habits and associated stress/strain on upstream supply chain.  
● Fablabs will be designed such that they can be operated in home, in shop or in small factories by anyone, unlike specialist machines used for paint customisation by trained technicians. Furthermore, the connectivity between our digital platform and fablabs will ensure real-time analysis of consumers’ demands and facilitate rapid production of particulate/liquid FMCG tailored to meet changing consumers’ needs. This will create business opportunities for new actors, especially SMEs, in the FMCG sector.  
Data Analytics Innovation Character: The main innovation of the data analytics module is the strong consideration of the human factors such as perception, culture, ontology, semantics and cognitive aspects. We will take into account all these complex parameters to define a “systemic design”. Moreover, a smart mix of algorithms and methods will be proposed while keeping the application fast and effective. The developed human-centred application will understand the needs, have the capacity to analyse and extract valuable information from any kind of data (image, text, video, process, thinking and audio …) and support the change of demands in order to ensure sustainability and scalability of our project.  
Modelling and Simulation Innovation Character: We will deliver innovation of DIY design by implementing the predictive simulation techniques (feed forward) based on multivariate analysis to predict flow, structural and thermal features, and the behaviour of ingredients in the formulation process at each process step. Currently, process systems in formulation fail to generate real-time data sets that enable predictive design, i.e current process optimisation models are not fit for on-demand customisation (feedback optimisation only). We will use Finite Element Analysis (FEA) and Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) to provide structural, thermal and fluid analysis of the formulation supported by a digital reproduction of the physical process to optimise cost and environmental performance, taking customer preferences and behaviour change into account. The fablab digital twin (a key innovation feature of our project) will make use of the fablab prototype equipment to ensure the virtual twin is correctly validated with the actual equipment. The developed open source LCA/LCCA modelling capability could be used with any soft matter FMCG to help consumers and SMEs make informed choices based on environmental performance. This is a new innovation to the market that is hoped to positively influence human behaviour before committing to the manufacture of goods, by increasing awareness of a product’s sustainability performance, which may drive a change in consumer purchasing habits.  
Blockchain Innovation Character: We will be the first publicly demonstrable application of blockchain within the formulation/FMCG manufacturing industry and value chain. According to venture capitalist William Mougayar, blockchain is “the second significant overlay on the internet, just as the web was the first layer back in 1990”. Within our platform, blockchain will enable managed access to valuable assets, such as intellectual property, new FMCG formulation innovations and payments, without the need for intermediaries such as IP Attorneys/Lawyers. Blockchain/DLT provides an irreversible, secure and time-stamped record, enabling our platform to be used for managing access to data and IP provided by multiple parties in a granular fashion. It can also provide evidence of use of company or individual IP (e.g. formulations, algorithms) and enforce IP licences through smart contracts.  
We will also pay close attention to the financial regulations emerging in the EU and G20 around the peer-to-peer transfer of value through blockchain. Whilst information currently travels instantly around the globe, the same cannot be said about business transactions and exchange of value. A typical international payment takes 3-5 days to settle, with ≥5% error rate and average cost of €37; €158.34 trillion worth of international payments are made annually at a combined cost of over €1.5 trillion p.a. If EU regulations change during our project to allow this functionality, and banks permit API integration with their services, payments for our project could be automatically facilitated by ‘smart contracts’ - code executed on a blockchain - and completed instantly, allowing FMCG value to move globally just like information.  
Fablab Innovation Character: The fablab is in essence an automated liquid or powder mixing and dispensing system that demonstrates self-cleaning and quality assurance. The novelty of such an approach will be embedded in advancing the limits of miniaturisation in terms of feeding, blending, dosing, and process analytics. While inline product testing using process analytical technology is itself not a new concept, achieving it within an interlinked complex formulation network around a geometrically constrained manufacturing space, combined with a limited analysis time will require innovative approaches. The integration of real-time process monitoring, complemented by decision making algorithms and self-learning neural networks will allow us to provide unrivalled ‘right first time’ small-scale manufacturing machines for soft matter FMCG.  
Patent/IP Search: A patent and IP landscape review performed by IP experts at STEF and STL suggest that our digital platform and fablab are novel and patentable; and will have no freedom to operate issues. The closest recent patents identified are:  
- Customized fast-moving consumer goods production system (WO2017014696A1): is the nearest patent to our solution but it does not propose a collaborative platform or smart algorithms for formulas.  
- Custom cosmetic blending machine (US8564778B1): is only devoted to specific cosmetic products and cannot be deployed for wider FMCG or other sectors. It also requires operation by experts.  
- Additive manufacturing for beauty products (WO2015132734A1): is an innovative system proposed by L'Oréal, however it is dedicated only for 3D printed nails.  
This IP analysis proves that there is no system that proposes a large collaborative solution for DIY design and production based on open technologies.  
IP held by our consortium: Digital Catapult holds IP on methodologies for reducing smart contract computation costs on the public Ethereum blockchain. IRIS holds IP on the IBD® and SHAREBOX platforms that will be adapted and used for our project. EFF holds IP for methods on decentralized smart agents. RDI holds IP for connected clothes and patent pending for connected medical device. DSC brings extensive knowhow on material and process modelling and simulation. CDY holds IP and knowhow on formulation manufacturing and related machines and related software. VTT holds IP on relevant miniaturised PAT spectrometers (e.g. NIR). P&G holds IP on detergent and soap/other FMGC manufacturing processes, machines and related modelling and simulation packages. STEF and CPI bring IP and knowhow on operation of small-scale automated equipment for diverse formulation applications. CETEMET holds patents for device and method for laying cables, device and method for processing electrical cables, and knot-tying head.

The first example of application of spatial ALD process has been the adoption of an Al2O3 layer as backside passivation film in crystalline silicon solar cell. Two companies, Levitech and SoLayTec focus on this technology to meet the industrial requirement of processing 3000 wafers/h, are currently selling commercial spatial ALD tools.1– 3 Flexible electronics such as light emitting diodes (OLED) as well as thin film photovoltaics are moving from the lab scale towards production, and several challenges are still to be tackled. Most of the materials used in these devices are sensitive to O2 and H2O, and therefore encapsulation strategies which are economically viable, compatible with high-throughput and low cost deposition methods on large area are strictly required. For OLEDs, water vapor transmission rates (WVTRs) as low as 10-6 g·m-2·day-1 are typically needed. For thin film photovoltaic modules the WVTR is expected to be less stringent (depending on type of by one or two orders of magnitude) but still far beyond the simple polymer encapsulation foils (1 g·m-2·day-1). Conventional solutions such as glass-glass encapsulation are not compatible with flexible PV applications, and suffer from relatively high weight. ALD layers such as Al2O3, multilayer stacks of Al2O3/TiO2, combination of inorganic/organic layers have been already proven in lab scale as valid solutions with excellent WVTR values. 

4–10 Spatial ALD approach combined with a roll to roll (R2R) line can potentially meet the requirement of high throughput low cost barrier/encapsulation. Spatial ALD Al2O3 50 nm thick film has been deposited on glass substrates of an industrially relevant size (370 × 470 mm2). The film exhibits a WVTR below the detection limit of the MOCON test (3 x 10-3 g·m-2·day-1 at 20°C/50 %RH).11 Spatial ALD Al2O3 and SnO2 have been demonstrated on a lab scale test as valid gas permeation barriers using oxygen plasma (PE-s-ALD) as oxidative source instead of the conventional water vapour. A 100nm thick Al2O3 layer deposited at 150 °C exhibits a WVTR of 5 x 10-5 g·m-2·day-1 (at 60°C/60 %RH, and of 2 x 10-4 g·m-2·day-1 (at 60°C/60 %RH) when deposited at 100°C.12 In the case of 110 nm SnO2 a value of 7 x 10-4 g·m-2·day-1 (at 60°C/60 %RH) has been measured for the SnO2 deposited at 150 °C, but one order of magnitude in WVTR has been achieved using a lower temperature (100 °C).13 TNO already tested spatial ALD layers on our own R2R pilot line. Depositing only 20 nm of Al2O3 by roll to roll atmospheric pressure spatial ALD on PET foil (30cm x 2m) without any pretreatment and/or planarization layer, drastically improves the WVTR value up to 2 x 10-5 g·m-2·day-1 (at 20°C/50 %RH) and 4 x 10-4 g·m-2·day-1 (at 60°C/90 %RH). Still improvements can be achieved developing “ad hoc” solutions/strategies. Advances proposed in POLYBIOSKIN for the development of a fully biodegradable diaper provided with a skin-compatible surface: A biodegradable (compostable) diaper prototype was produced using more than 90% of renewable bio-based materials. The skin compatibility of our POLYBIOSKIN diaper surface (topsheet) was demonstrated in vitro using advanced cell cultures. The positive results confirming the skin compatibility of our POLYBIOSKIN diaper are due to the bio-based polymers used to manufacture the topsheet and to a finish treatment based on chitin derivatives, in particular on complexes based on chitin nanofibrils (CN) and nanolignin (LG). In general, PLA-based blend films were demonstrated by INSTM to be more compatible with skin than polyolefins, boosting the use of such bioplastics in applications in contact with skin. The different parts of the diaper/femcare prototypes resulted degradable in compost with a kinetic compatible with industrial composting, with the exception of the super-absorbent powder, which showed a slow biodegradation behavior. Anyway, the full sanitary fem care prototypes disintegrated efficiently in compost. 

Advances proposed in POLYBIOSKIN for the development of fully biodegradable and bioactive facial beauty masks based on biopolymers provided with a skin-compatible surface: In POLYBIOSKIN, we considered two different technologies for preparing beauty mask prototypes. At M18, several possibilities developed by INSTM, UGENT/BIOINICIA, TEXOL/FIBROLINE, and ARMINES were analyzed, and the beauty mask based on pullulan incorporating functional powder, produced by UGENT and BIOINICIA, was selected because of its peculiar properties (potentially commercial as a dry product, without preservatives, nanostructured, and soluble in water for a very efficient release of active substances, among others). The film-based beauty masks investigated by INSTM were made using natural and sustainable resources and by the combination of two novel technologies, electrahydrodynamic and powder impregnation, which allow the generation of bio-based wound dressing with antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, and skin repairing properties. A skin equivalent using hMSCs (as dermal fibroblasts) and HaCaT (as keratinocytes) cultured on a gelatin-alginate scaffold was generated to test the wound dressing in an advanced in vitro system emulating the skin. We were able to produce and upscale a bio-based wound dressing prototype based on the combination of electrohydrodynamic and powder impregnation technologies. In vitro tests are currently being carried out to ensure the advanced performance of our product. From our perspective, the scientific and technological achievements reached in POLYBIOSKIN will impact both the markets of application and society. In our UNIPI unit, we prepared a gelatine sponge incorporating fibroblasts (obtained by differentiating hMSCs), embodied in alginate hydrogel and covered by keratinocytes (HaCaT cells) as a skin model. This structure reproduces the double layer of the skin, dermis and epidermis. We cut a wound with a scalpel in this skin analogue and inserted the POLYBIOSKIN wound dressing to evaluate the cell migration inside the fibres. The results with histology and SEM showed that the cells migrated from the skin equivalent to cover and colonize the wound dressing, providing fibrous structures at the nano and micro scale with interconnecting pores, resembling the natural extracellular matrix (ECM) in tissues, and showing a high potential to facilitate the regeneration of functional tissues. Although the technology is mainly used at a laboratory scale, we managed to upscale electrospun PHA rolls as a carrier base for the wound dressing material, generating a compostable layer showing positive in vitro results. The cooperative interaction of University of Westminster/Sheffield for novel PHA production, UGENT (with the support of INSTM) for setting up a reproducible electrospinning method of these specific polymers, our upscaling capability, and the impregnation technology of FIBROLINE altogether led to the obtainment of a novel wound dressing (specifically the bioactive part) which revealed marked immunomodulatory and inherent antibacterial properties (neither due to antibiotic release, nor very aggressive like silver), thus apt for being used in chronic wounds. In particular, we could impregnate homogeneously very low porosity substrates like nanostructured textile-based wound dressing with very low content of bioactive powder like CN-LG complexes for wound healing and beauty masks. We also observed homogeneous distribution regarding the impregnation of the Bio-SAP powder in non-woven substrates. We upscaled this technology to reach the final prototypes, which gave positive results and showed efficacy in the biomedical market. This technology was very versatile and very much applicable on nonwovens as they are generally provided with a suitable porosity. Beyond the State of the art in validation of cosmetic products, we can obtain cosmetic products via electrospinning, a novel and cheap technology, which provides a great potential but has not been very exploited so far, due to initial difficulty in upscaling. However, electrospinning is becoming more and more popular in the last years, and many efforts have permitted it to reach an industrially relevant scale. In terms of the cosmetic product, the face beauty mask generated within POLYBIOSKIN is made with a two-step process that allows the incorporation of most bioactive ingredients in a dry form. In this way, we overcome the market of pre-wetted masks. Indeed, the current pre-wet nonwovens in cosmetic products soak the carrier with the active ingredient and therefore do not guarantee that active ingredients are properly distributed in the carrier mask. Moreover, storing and selling wet masks poses the issue of maintaining the function of the active ingredients without degradation, which implies the use of preservatives. In this sense, the facial beauty mask generated within POLYBIOSKIN allows for an adequate distribution of the active ingredient across the carrier as well as a high skin penetration due to the high surface area of nanofibres. This can be reflected in the data from the human panel study, in which the in vivo topical treatment significantly displayed antiaging effects after 2 and 4 weeks of topical application in human volunteers through significant reduction of forehead and crow's feet wrinkles (area, length, and depth), and significant increase of moisturizing levels. As well as this, a test at the end of the study showed an overall positive consumer acceptance, suggesting that the qualitative data was in good agreement with the quantitative data. Additionally, regarding dermatological surveillance, the beauty mask showed good skin compatibility and may claim “Dermatologically tested,” “Clinically Tested,” and “Tolerance Tested.” The electrospinning technique consists of a simple and cost-effective process leading to fibrous structures, which can be very efficient in biomedical applications. The meshes we generated can provide fibrous structures at the nano and micro scale with interconnecting pores, resembling the natural extracellular matrix (ECM) in tissues, and showing a high potential to facilitate the regeneration of functional tissues. The problems that have slowed down the translation of clinical electrospun nanofibre products into the market are associated with two main different aspects: (1) the manufacturing and technical part. In fact, although highly scalable, the industrial throughput is (still) not competitive. In biomedical applications, due to the reduced area of the devices, this problem is more easily solvable; (2) the difficulty to achieve the legal standards posed by the regulatory bodies for these novel technologies which exploit state-of-the-art research, such as the devices containing living cells and/or bioactive molecules. For the first aspect, the thin electrospun layer and the small area needed by wound dressings resulted in an industrially affordable option for further scale-up. For the second aspect, thanks to the support of Prof. Sheila McNeil (AB member), we were able to better understand how to possibly position the POLYBIOSKIN wound dressing in the market and how it could be considered from a regulatory point of view (e.g., medical device class III, not advanced therapy medicinal product (ATMP), if the effect is physico-mechanical and local, namely not due to drug/molecule/particle release). The Global Bioactive Wound Care Market is growing very fast due to rising incidences of road accidents & injuries and increasing conditions that impair wound healing capabilities. Non-healing wounds remain a challenge for the development of dressing materials. Multiple functions and specific properties are required for future wound care products to provide a suitable micro-environment. In POLYBIOSKIN, we aim to address this growing need with an effective dressing made using natural and sustainable resources and by the combination of two novel technologies, electrahydrodynamic and powder impregnation, which allow the generation of bio-based wound dressing with antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, and skin repairing properties. We used Fibroline to incorporate different wound dressings tested using human keratinocytes (HaCaT cells) by INSTM. A skin equivalent using hMSCs (as dermal fibroblasts) and HaCaT (as keratinocytes) cultured on a gelatin scaffold was generated to test the wound dressing in an advanced in vitro system emulating the skin. We were able to produce and upscale a bio-based wound dressing prototype based on the combination of electrohydrodynamic and powder impregnation technologies. In vitro tests are currently being carried out to ensure the advanced performance of our product. From our perspective, the scientific and technological achievements reached in POLYBIOSKIN will impact both the markets of application and society. In all the dissemination and stakeholder events we attended, we felt the arising interest not just in shifting to products with less environmental impact but also in finding bio-based solutions for personal care applications that are healthier and have clear scientifically demonstrated functional properties. Hereafter, we provide a detailed description of the progress beyond the state of the art (SoA) achieved by our consortium.

Use case 1: From Captured CO2 to low-carbon Olefins: An Integrated CCU Value Chain Demonstration Enabled by Green Hydrogen Production – The Petrochemical Cluster Brief description: This use case demonstrates a highly intensified CCU value chain that transforms CO2 contained in our industrial flue gas into low-carbon ethylene and propylene through a streamlined two-step process. By directly integrating Vacuum-assisted Calcium Looping (V-CaL) capture and purification with a pilot-scale Sorption-Enhanced Reactor featuring Continuous Sorbent Feeding (CSF), we eliminate intermediate logistics and energy-intensive processing while overcoming thermodynamic equilibrium limitations to achieve higher conversion rates. The process is supplied with green hydrogen from a 25 kW Anion Exchange Membrane (AEM) electrolyser developed by TRSA, providing a decentralized and cost-effective hydrogen source. Implemented at our TOR industrial site, this integrated architecture delivers a scalable and sustainable route from our industrial emissions to chemical building blocks. The resulting olefin products are rigorously validated by ORL to confirm compliance with petrochemical-grade specifications for downstream applications such as polymer production. Actors involved (and their role): TOR (CO2 Emitter); TRSA (CO2 Capture, Conversion and Hydrogen Supply); ORL (End User and Product Validation) Business, Productivity, Environmental & Technological goals: ■ Technological: To validate the operational stability of a TRL 6 integrated pilot combining V-CaL capture, AEM electrolysis, and CSF conversion under dynamic industrial loads. ■ Environmental: To achieve around 30% efficiency improvement in our process of light olefin production compared to conventional Methanol to Olefins and steam cracking while reducing our current olefin fossil-dependence and carbon footprint. ■ Business: To de-risk our investment for large-scale CCU deployment by establishing the Techno-Economic Viability of a low-emission ethylene and propylene supply chain. We aim to demonstrate a 30% reduction in total operational expenses when compared to Methanol-to-Olefin and steam cracking benchmarks, achieved through process intensification and the integration of low-cost AEM electrolysis. Motivating scenario (background, problem, objectives): The European chemical sector faces a dual challenge: the urgent mandate to decarbonize and the need to reduce reliance on imported fossil feedstocks. Our current light olefin production via steam cracking is carbon-intensive and highly sensitive to volatile gas markets. Simultaneously, our industrial CO2 emissions represent a latent feedstock currently treated as waste. This use case addresses these challenges by coupling the ceramic industry (Emitter) with the petrochemical industry (End-User) through an innovative catalytic bridge, transforming a liability (emissions) into a strategic asset (ethylene/propylene). Main Tasks (technological solutions): The main tasks of this use case focus on the implementation and integration of key technological solutions across our CO2 value chain. First, we will capture CO2 from our industrial flue gas using vacuum-assisted calcium looping, ensuring with a single step a concentrated and suitable stream for downstream utilisation. The captured CO2 will then be converted into light olefins (ethylene and propylene) in a pilot-scale sorption-enhanced reactor with continuous sorbent feeding, enabling improved conversion efficiency through process intensification. In parallel, we will produce renewable hydrogen using a 25 kW AEM electrolyzer and supply it to the reactor under controlled conditions. The overall system will be designed, constructed, and integrated to ensure stable and continuous operation, including gas conditioning, process control, and safety management. Finally, a Product Quality Benchmarking task will be led by ORL to verify the purity and grade of the produced olefins against ASTM/ISO industrial standards. This includes assessing the impact of trace impurities on downstream polymerisation processes, ensuring a 'drop-in' compatibility with our existing European petrochemical infrastructure. The data generated during our initial validation campaigns will subsequently be used to develop virtual process simulations of a circular industrial symbiosis scenario, in which ORL’s flue gas streams act as CO2 emitters and ORL itself as the downstream end-user of the produced olefins. Use case 2: Circular Mineralisation: Closing the Industrial Loop via Captured CO2-to-Lithium Carbonate Conversion for Advanced Ceramic Manufacturing – The Mineral/Ceramic Hub Brief description: This use case demonstrates a circular industrial symbiosis model by converting captured CO2 into Li2CO3, a critical raw material for the ceramic and battery sectors. CO2 captured via V-CaL at our TOR industrial facility will be supplied to ACC, where it will undergo a proprietary mineralisation and conversion process. This transformation utilizes CO2 as a secondary feedstock to produce high-purity Li2CO3. To complete the circular value chain, the produced Lithium Carbonate will be returned to TOR for internal validation and integration into our industrial production lines (e.g., as a fluxing agent in ceramic frits or glazes). This use case validates the technical and economic feasibility of "Carbon-to-Product" pathways that reduce both our industrial emissions and the dependency on imported mineral carbonates. Use case 1: From Captured CO2 to low-carbon Olefins: An Integrated CCU Value Chain Demonstration Enabled by Green Hydrogen Production – The Petrochemical Cluster Brief description: This use case demonstrates a highly intensified CCU value chain that transforms CO2 contained in our industrial flue gas into low-carbon ethylene and propylene through a streamlined two-step process. By directly integrating Vacuum-assisted Calcium Looping (V-CaL) capture and purification with a pilot-scale Sorption-Enhanced Reactor featuring Continuous Sorbent Feeding (CSF), we eliminate intermediate logistics and energy-intensive processing while overcoming thermodynamic equilibrium limitations to achieve higher conversion rates. The process is supplied with green hydrogen from a 25 kW Anion Exchange Membrane (AEM) electrolyser developed by TRSA, providing a decentralized and cost-effective hydrogen source. Implemented at our TOR industrial site, this integrated architecture delivers a scalable and sustainable route from our industrial emissions to chemical building blocks. The resulting olefin products are rigorously validated by ORL to confirm compliance with petrochemical-grade specifications for downstream applications such as polymer production. Actors involved (and their role): TOR (CO2 Emitter); TRSA (CO2 Capture, Conversion and Hydrogen Supply); ORL (End User and Product Validation) Business, Productivity, Environmental & Technological goals: ■ Technological: To validate the operational stability of a TRL 6 integrated pilot combining V-CaL capture, AEM electrolysis, and CSF conversion under dynamic industrial loads. ■ Environmental: To achieve around 30% efficiency improvement in our process of light olefin production compared to conventional Methanol to Olefins and steam cracking while reducing our current olefin fossil-dependence and carbon footprint. ■ Business: To de-risk the investment for large-scale CCU deployment by establishing the Techno-Economic Viability of a low-emission ethylene and propylene supply chain. We aim to demonstrate a 30% reduction in total operational expenses when compared to Methanol-to-Olefin and steam cracking benchmarks, achieved through process intensification and the integration of low-cost AEM electrolysis. Motivating scenario (background, problem, objectives): The European chemical sector faces a dual challenge: the urgent mandate to decarbonize and the need to reduce reliance on imported fossil feedstocks. Our current light olefin production via steam cracking is carbon-intensive and highly sensitive to volatile gas markets. Simultaneously, our industrial CO2 emissions represent a latent feedstock currently treated as waste. This use case addresses these challenges by coupling the ceramic industry (Emitter) with the petrochemical industry (End-User) through an innovative catalytic bridge, transforming a liability (emissions) into a strategic asset (ethylene/propylene). Main Tasks (technological solutions): The main tasks of this use case focus on the implementation and integration of key technological solutions across the CO2 value chain. First, we will capture CO2 from our industrial flue gas using vacuum-assisted calcium looping, ensuring with a single step a concentrated and suitable stream for downstream utilisation. The captured CO2 will then be converted into light olefins (ethylene and propylene) in a pilot-scale sorption-enhanced reactor with continuous sorbent feeding, enabling improved conversion efficiency through process intensification. In parallel, we will produce renewable hydrogen using a 25 kW AEM electrolyzer and supply it to the reactor under controlled conditions. The overall system will be designed, constructed, and integrated to ensure stable and continuous operation, including gas conditioning, process control, and safety management. Finally, a Product Quality Benchmarking task will be led by ORL to verify the purity and grade of the produced olefins against ASTM/ISO industrial standards. This includes assessing the impact of trace impurities on downstream polymerisation processes, ensuring a 'drop-in' compatibility with our existing European petrochemical infrastructure. The data generated during the initial validation campaigns will subsequently be used to develop virtual process simulations of a circular industrial symbiosis scenario, in which ORL’s flue gas streams act as CO2 emitters and ORL itself as the downstream end-user of the produced olefins.
